content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To    :    Advice Files 87100, 87103(a), 87103(c)         Date  :  Jan. 29, 1982


From  :  FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
         Diane

Subject:    Telephone Advice to Jack Heck, Member of Mining
            & Geology Board


FACTS:

     I spoke with both Doug Sprague, Executive Officer of
the State Mining and Geology Board, and Jack Heck, a member
of the Board, who provided me with the following facts.

     Mr. Heck is employed by Kaiser Sand and Gravel company,
and he has an investment of over $1,000 in Koppers Corp.,
the parent company of Kaiser. Koppers has four wholly owned
subsidiaries including Kaiser which are in the sand and
gravel business in California.

     Pursuant to Public Resources Code Sections 2761, et seq.,
the Board makes what are called "designation" decisions.
Generally this type of decision identifies the mineral resources
which are necessary in a particular region; the designation
is then included in the local jurisdiction's general plan.
If an area is designated as needing a certain mineral resource,
it makes it much easier for a mining company to obtain a
mining permit for that resource. The statutes provide for
an appeal process to the Board if such a permit is denied by
the local jurisdiction and otherwise limit the discretion of
the local jurisdiction in making certain land use decisions
involving mining.

QUESTIONS:

     Mr. Heck asked whether he would have a conflict of
interest in any of the following situations:

     1.  If a designation decision pertained to property
         owned by Kaiser;

Advice Files 87100, 87103(a), 87103(c)
January 29, 1982
Page Two


2.  If a designation decision pertained to property
owned by another subsidiary of Koppers in the sand and gravel
business;

3.  If a designation decision pertained to property
owned by a competitor of Kaiser.

ADVICE GIVEN:

I told him that he would have to disqualify himself on
designation decisions involving Kaiser-owned property. The
decision would affect whether Kaiser could mine, and thus it
had a material effect on a source of income to him.

If a decision involved property owned by another sub-
sidiary of Koppers, I said he would have to disqualify himself
based on his investment in Koppers.

On decisions involving the property of a competitor, I
said whether he would have to disqualify himself depended on
whether there would be a material financial effect on Kaiser
or Koppers as a result of the decisions. For example, if
only a limited number of designations could be granted,
whether a competitor received a designation would affect an
application by Kaiser. Therefore, it will have to be resolved
on a case-by-case basis. I indicated that a material effect
on the competitor alone was not enough to require disqualification;
there would also have to be a material effect on either
Kaiser or Koppers.

I also explained to Mr. Heck what it meant to "participate"
in a decision, that it covered everything from private dis-
cussions with other Board members to actually voting on a
matter.

DMF:km

sections.question: 1. If a designation decision pertained to property owned by Kaiser; 2. If a designation decision pertained to property owned by another subsidiary of Koppers in the sand and gravel business; 3. If a designation decision pertained to property owned by a competitor of Kaiser.
sections.conclusion: Mr. Heck would have to disqualify himself on designation decisions involving Kaiser-owned property because the decision would affect whether Kaiser could mine and thus had a material effect on his source of income. If a decision involved property owned by another Koppers subsidiary, he would have to disqualify himself on his investment in Koppers. On decisions involving a competitor's property, disqualification would depend on whether there would be a material financial effect on Kaiser or Koppers as a result; a material effect on the competitor alone was not enough to require disqualification. Participation includes everything from private discussions to voting.

sections.question_synthetic: When must Jack Heck, a member of the Mining and Geology Board with financial interests in Kaiser and Koppers sand and gravel companies, disqualify himself from designation decisions affecting Kaiser-owned property, other Koppers subsidiaries, and competitor properties?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Heck must disqualify himself from decisions affecting Kaiser or other Koppers subsidiaries due to material financial effects on his income sources. For competitor properties, disqualification is required only if the decision would materially affect Kaiser or Koppers, not merely the competitor.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document has significant OCR quality issues with missing/corrupted text throughout. Questions and conclusions are reconstructible from context but some specific language may be inaccurate. The facts section is heavily damaged but core scenario is discernible. Advice section is more legible and extracted with higher confidence.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. If a designation decision pertained to property owned by Kaiser; 2. If a designation decision pertained to property owned by another subsidiary of Koppers in the sand and gravel business; 3. If a designation decision pertained to property owned by a competitor of Kaiser.

CONCLUSION: Mr. Heck would have to disqualify himself on designation decisions involving Kaiser-owned property because the decision would affect whether Kaiser could mine and thus had a material effect on his source of income. If a decision involved property owned by another Koppers subsidiary, he would have to disqualify himself on his investment in Koppers. On decisions involving a competitor's property, disqualification would depend on whether there would be a material financial effect on Kaiser or Koppers as a result; a material effect on the competitor alone was not enough to require disqualification. Participation includes everything from private discussions to voting.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises Jack Heck, a Mining and Geology Board member with financial interests in Kaiser and Koppers sand and gravel companies, on disqualification requirements for designation decisions affecting properties owned by Kaiser, other Koppers subsidiaries, and competitors.
